index.hero.title: Rocket Equation (Tsiolkovsky) - Calculator & Intuition
index.hero.subtitle: An educational, zero-build page for understanding how rocket mass ratio relates to achievable Δv.
index.equation.title: The Tsiolkovsky rocket equation
index.equation.figcaption: Where Δv is change in velocity, v<sub>e</sub> is effective exhaust velocity, m<sub>0</sub> is initial mass, and m<sub>f</sub> is final mass.
index.explanation.title: Explanation
index.explanation.p1: The Tsiolkovsky rocket equation tells you how much change in velocity a rocket can gain (called Δv, pronounced "delta-v") from burning its propellant. Think of Δv as the rocket's "speed budget" for a maneuver.
index.explanation.p2: This equation is an idealized physics model: it assumes the engine's performance stays the same during the burn and it ignores losses like air drag and gravity pulling you down while you burn. Real missions have those extra losses, but the rocket equation is still the starting point for understanding what's possible.
index.explanation.dv_desc: The change in velocity the rocket can produce by burning fuel, measured in meters per second (m/s). Bigger Δv means the rocket can speed up (or slow down) more.
index.explanation.isp_desc: A measure of how efficient an engine is, measured in seconds (s). Higher Isp means the engine gets more "push" out of the same amount of propellant.
index.explanation.g0_desc: A constant used to connect Isp to exhaust speed: g0 = 9.80665 m/s². It's based on Earth's gravity at sea level.
index.explanation.m0_desc: The rocket's mass before the burn, fully fueled (in kilograms, kg).
index.explanation.mf_desc: The rocket's mass after the burn, with the propellant used up (in kilograms, kg). This is sometimes called "dry mass" or "empty mass."
index.explanation.p3: Many calculators use an equivalent form of the equation: Δv = Isp × g0 × ln(m0/mf). Here, ln means the natural logarithm, and the fraction m0/mf is the mass ratio (how many times heavier the rocket is when full compared to when empty).
index.calculator.title: Calculator
index.calculator.legend: Compute Δv from Isp and masses
index.calculator.error.isp: Isp must be a positive number.
index.calculator.error.m0: Wet mass (m0) must be a positive number.
index.calculator.error.mf: Dry mass (mf) must be a positive number.
index.calculator.error.mass_order: Wet mass must be greater than dry mass.
index.calculator.error.invalid: Inputs produced an invalid Δv. Please double-check your numbers.
index.example.title: Worked example
index.example.card_title: Falcon 9 Stage 1 (Block 5) - plug in real numbers
index.example.result_note: (This is the ideal value from the equation for these inputs.)
index.example.p1: What does ~7.8 km/s mean? Δv is a rocket's "speed budget" in an ideal world. A value around 7-8 km/s is in the same ballpark as the speed needed for low Earth orbit (about 7.8 km/s), but real launches need more than orbital speed because gravity and air drag steal some of that budget while you climb.
index.example.p2: That's why Falcon 9 needs a second stage: Stage 1 provides a huge push early on, then Stage 2 continues accelerating above most of the atmosphere to reach orbit.
index.example.action.load: Load these numbers into the calculator
index.intuition.title: Intuition
index.intuition.p1: The tricky part of rockets is that fuel has to lift (and accelerate) other fuel. Early in the burn, you're heavy, so each second of thrust has to speed up a lot of mass. Later in the burn, you're lighter, so the same engine can change your velocity faster. This "snowball" effect is why adding more fuel helps, but with diminishing returns.
index.intuition.p2: The natural log (ln) captures those diminishing returns. If Δv is proportional to ln(m0/mf), then to get double the Δv you need to square the mass ratio: doubling Δv means doubling the log, and doubling a log means squaring what's inside. That's why rockets often use staging (dropping empty tanks/engines to reduce mass) - it helps reset the mass ratio - but staging is out of scope for this simple calculator.
index.footer.prefix: MVP skeleton page for Rocket Equation education ·
designer.hero.title: Multi-stage rocket designer
designer.hero.subtitle: Experiment with staged rockets using the shared core library: tune masses, Isp, thrust, and payload to see how the stack changes in real time.
designer.intro.title: Start from a sourced two-stage example
designer.intro.body: The default stack uses Falcon 9 Full Thrust stage numbers from issue #24's advisory comment so this page stays educational without inventing extra aerospace data.
designer.controls.title: Vehicle inputs
designer.controls.note: Use 1 to 6 stages. Inputs update the outputs, capability label, and chart on every change.
designer.summary.title: Live stack results
designer.warning.underpowered: Warning: Stage 1 initial TWR is {twr}, so the rocket is underpowered at liftoff.
designer.chart.title: Velocity vs burned propellant fraction
designer.chart.note: The chart shows how cumulative ideal Δv grows as each stage burns through its propellant.
designer.chart.svg_title: Velocity gain versus burned propellant fraction
designer.chart.svg_desc: A line chart showing cumulative ideal delta-v as the rocket burns propellant across all stages.
designer.chart.figcaption: X-axis: burned propellant fraction. Y-axis: cumulative ideal Δv in meters per second.
designer.chart.error: Fix the highlighted inputs to restore the chart.
designer.error.non_negative: {label} must be greater than or equal to 0.
designer.error.fix_inputs: Fix the highlighted inputs to update the results and chart.
compare.hero.title: Real launcher compare
compare.hero.subtitle: Start from cited launcher data, compare the ideal stack to a cited mission requirement, and explore how stage-by-stage what-if changes shift the outcome.
compare.preset.title: Choose a launcher preset
compare.preset.label: Launcher preset
compare.summary.title: Computed vs cited Δv
compare.editor.title: Launcher editor
compare.sources.title: Sources
compare.sources.note: Every URL below comes directly from the advisory comment in issue #24.
compare.slider.label: What-if Isp slider
compare.error.fix_inputs: Fix the highlighted inputs to recompute the launcher comparison.
compare.note.payload: {note} The payload field is derived at runtime from the cited mission Δv and the sourced stage numbers, so slider and input changes can move it away from the reference comparison.
compare.launcher.falcon9.name: Falcon 9 v1.2 expendable
compare.launcher.falcon9.note: Issue #24 notes that SpaceX does not publish a single total mission Δv, so this preset compares the ideal stack to the cited surface-to-GTO requirement.
compare.launcher.saturn_v.name: Saturn V
compare.launcher.saturn_v.note: Issue #24 recommends comparing Saturn V to the cited TLI requirement assembled from the BYU and ESA sources.
compare.launcher.long_march_5.name: Long March 5
compare.launcher.long_march_5.note: Issue #24 recommends modeling the four boosters in parallel with the core; this MVP serializes them as a first stage while keeping the cited stage numbers unchanged.
compare.launcher.soyuz_21b.name: Soyuz-2.1b
compare.launcher.soyuz_21b.note: Issue #24 recommends modeling the four boosters in parallel with the core; this MVP serializes them as a first stage while preserving the cited per-stage values.
designer_v2.hero.title: Rocket Designer v2
designer_v2.hero.subtitle: Build a more realistic launch stack with sourced engines, boosters, structural-index checks, and the shared mission-analysis core.
designer_v2.intro.title: A stage-card UI over the tested physics model
designer_v2.intro.body: This page loads the cited engine catalog from docs/data/engines.json, feeds your draft into docs/lib/designer_v2/physics.js, and keeps the summary panel aligned with the same preset configs covered by the physics tests.
designer_v2.controls.title: Vehicle builder
designer_v2.controls.note: Use presets as starting points, then tune stages, boosters, nozzle choices, and tank fractions. Any red structural badge disables Analyze until you fix the stack.
designer_v2.controls.nozzle_auto: This engine only exposes one nozzle profile here.
designer_v2.controls.solid_booster_note: Solid mode uses the cited SLS SRB blob from the engine catalog.
designer_v2.card.boosters_inactive: Set booster count above 0 to add a parallel booster phase.
designer_v2.card.fairing_note: Top-stage dry mass includes the default 1,500 kg fairing unless the loaded preset overrides it.
designer_v2.summary.title: Sticky mission summary
designer_v2.summary.invalid: Fix the highlighted inputs to update the summary.
designer_v2.summary.blocked: Analyze is disabled until every structural badge is green or yellow.
designer_v2.summary.booster_jettison: Booster jettison Δv: {dv} km/s; Stage 1 continues with {propellant} t propellant.
designer_v2.error.engine_required: Choose an engine from the catalog.
designer_v2.error.fix_inputs: Fix the highlighted inputs to continue.
designer_v2.error.analysis_failed: The current draft could not be analyzed.
designer_v2.error.catalog_fetch: The engine catalog could not be loaded.
designer_v2.warning.no_liftoff: The rocket will not lift off.
designer_v2.warning.vacuum_at_sea_level: {label}: vacuum nozzle selected at sea level, so mixed launch performance is used.
designer_v2.warning.vacuum_only_launch: {label}: vacuum-only engine used at sea level.
designer_v2.warning.sl_on_upper_stage: {label}: sea-level nozzle selected on an upper stage.
